February 3 - 9,  2026
Device Testing
Reproducibilty
We check the reproducibility of the new sequence in all V2 devices. We run it with injection, vial, and full sequence.
Run #1 - #8 used injected sequences (IS6110 6600 copies and Human 6600 copies).
Run #9 - #12 used vial sequence with dried chip without heating and sonicating (IS6110 1M copies, MSM 1M copies and Human 1M cp).
Run #13 - #20 used full sequence with heating and sonicating
		The results : 
- Run with Injected sequence show standard deviation (stdev) below than 1 on most channel except channel 4, because we got weak amplification at Run#2
-  Run with vial and full sequence shows all channels have stdev below than 1.
-  We got some aborted in full sequence because the latex is leaking, so we must rework the latex first before running the process.

Reproducibility with gBlock
The next day we continued the reproducibility test using device 003, 005, 006. In this test we used the gBlock of MSM, IS6110 , and Human with 1M copies and we used the full sequence set up. 
We still got several aborted runs because the latex leaked even after we retouch and used a latex cover. So that’s why in several runs we replaced the latex with a new one and successfully completed the run.

Reproducibility with MSM bacteria
The next day we continued the reproducibility test, in this test we used the MSM bacteria with 100k CFU/600uL and 1M copies/600 uL of IS6110 and Human gBlock. The results when using the MSM bacteria the stdev can below than 1 between run and devices, and also in device 3 at channel 3 the ct shows around 17 in most of run it’s different from other devices that makes higher stdev than other channels.


Dry Chip Development
Carried out production of Dry Chips for batches 662 and 663 and performed quality control. Both batches showed good quality curves; however, there was amplicon contamination. Therefore, we conducted a general cleaning of the production laboratory on February 5, 2026.

Plan
Test the stability of the Dry Chip at 80°C for 30 minutes after 1 month of storage. The test will be conducted on February 13, 2026.
If the chips remain stable after one month, we will begin preparing 36 chips for a one-year stability study. We will test 3 chips per month.
Test the tolerance limits of the baking temperature. We will test 5°C below and 5°C above the set point, so the temperatures to be tested are 75°C, 80°C, and 85°C.
Prepare Dry Chips for in-house testing.
Prepare Dry Chips for testing with RSPAW and R2D2 samples.


DSBio LOD with TS Previous results showed that DSBio could detect low copies of gblock (up to 5 cp/uL), yet the negative control also showed amplification. We then retested it again after making new reagent stocks and the results showed that we still had persistent amplicons (the amplicons produced having the same or similar peak with the target (IS6110) by the melt curves) and the same pattern was observed when the addition of TS had inhibitory effects towards DSB performance as previous results. Because of those persistent amplicons, we did general lab cleaning the next day.

Assay concentrations affecting tongue swab sample curves (FUSA)
Increasing assay concentration in the reaction increased signal to background for control and tongue swab reactions. Just an example of another basic reagent component that we could adjust to potentially improve tongue swab PCR reactions, especially at low target concentrations. (link)

Testing PCR enhancers with tongue swab samples (FUSA)
Performed a couple of quick experiments with PEG or carnitine added to the PCR reaction. No clear improvement in this first experiment, but just an example of additives we could try later. (link). Previous carnitine result from the DE project with saliva (link).
 

January 20, 2026 - MgCl2 concentrations affecting tongue swab sample curves (FUSA)
As I mentioned in the January 19 Scientist Meeting below, I have done some testing in FUSA in which I have evaluated different concentrations of MgCl2 (1.25 mM to 3.75 mM) in the PCR reaction with low copy target (100 cp/rxn) tongue swab and control samples. At mid and higher level MgCl2 concentrations, Taqman curves were more robust with early Cts, but high concentrations did result in higher ratios of non-specific primer dimer amplification. See Experiments 3 through 5 (link). It is worth evaluating MgCl2 concentrations with our tongue swab device and low target concentration. DSBio example from H2 2025 (link).

January 12-19,  2026
Device Testing
Preliminary test, MSM with HS DSBio
Since we did not have data of MSM cells using HS DsBio, we tried to collect this data using liquid set up.
We spike 30uL to swab stick from concentrations : 
5 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 6750 cfu / 40 uL.
2.5 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 3375 cfu / 40uL.
1.25 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 1687 cfu / 40uL.
The mechanism is we heat and sonicate the sample in the first, then we take 27uL of sample and add with 13uL of formulation of assay and polymerase, after that the solution is injected into the channel and run in the 005 device.


BSL-2 inhouse testingThe first objective is to test MSM cells that spiked to TS and sputum samples. In this test, 20 uL from 5 M CFU/mL MSM cells was spiked onto tongue swab (100K CFU on TS sample). Afterwards, the tongue swab is going through a full process in device 005 with 600 uL resuspension buffer. And then, est final concentration is around 40/600*100,000 = 6,666 CFU/channel. On the other hand for sputum test, 100 uL from MSM stock (16 M CFU/mL) was added to sputum 17 (saliva) which est vol around 500 uL. est final concentration of MSM after blank flock swab dipped to saliva is around 80K - 100K CFU/swab. What we observed from this test, so far: HS DSBio perform better than the ftaq to detect MSM and the curves were sigmoid while using blank flock swab (ctrl), but HS DSBio curves start sloping when we introduce TS and sputum sample. Another thing is, it is nice to see that the fTaq could detect MSM cells, because from the previous data, when we try it with liquid set up with MSM cell, ftaq couldn't detect it (the curve was almost flat, data: 12/03/2025; 12/05/2025; 12/11/2025)

The second objective is to test IS6110 gBlock 100K copies/600 uL that spiked into vial tube. Run #1 and #2, run as a control without TS or sputum, all channel were amplified on DSbio and Ftaq. Run #3 and #4, added TS sample to vial, the result shows on Ftaq has weak amplification while DSbio shows strong curve. Run #5 and 6 (contains TS samples that was dipped to sputum that was spiked with gblock) were aborted early in the cycling process. The sample used in run#5 was visibly more viscous compared to normal TS samples, which could've caused the run to be aborted since it hindered the liquid movement. In run#7 and *8 (blank floqswab dipped into saliva-purulent sputum, fTaq did not exhibit any activity and DSBio produced curve with poor quality for the IS6110 assay. Both polymerase performed well for the human assay. In run#9 and #10 (blank floqswab dipped into saliva), both polymerase performed well and matched it's control performance

Figure 1. Trend of a-value

The drying parameter 50C 80 min shows good stability in two weeks. The CT and b-values showed low standard deviation across both IS6110-FAM and Human-ROX channels, indicating good stability after drying (Table 1). In contrast, the a-value showed higher variability, especially at early time points. However, as shown in Table 2 and Figure 1, the average a-value decreased sharply from day 0 to day 6, then became relatively stable after that for both DSBio HS and fTaq. Although some fluctuation was still observed, the overall trend suggests that the dry chip maintained acceptable performance for up to two weeks. 

80C 30 mins
Since the previous experiment showed no significant difference in performance between chips baked at 50°C for 80 minutes and those baked at 80°C for 30 minutes, we repeated the stability test at 80°C for 30 minutes. New testing was conducted on day 0 and day 1 and will be continued for up to two weeks. The 50°C for 80 minutes parameter is still feasible for producing 36 chips per day; however, the 80°C for 30 minutes condition provides greater time flexibility, especially  for Quality Control or to enable a further increase in production quantity.

However, the a-value from the new stability test at 50°C for 80 minutes still needs further observation, because only one replicate is available after Bayu’s repair (day 6). January 6, 2026 - Using anneal/extend temp to improve sensitivity
In the FUSA meeting today, we discussed a quick test that could be done to potentially reduce non-specific amplification and increase sensitivity with low target concentrations. We recommend testing increasing the anneal/extend temperature to reduce non-specific amplification (could be relevant to both fTaq and DSBio reactions). Below is an example gradient PCR experiment that I performed last month with fTaq and 100 copies of IS6110 gBlock and Evagreen. In this experiment (69C-circles and 65C-crosses) yielded more robust and target-specific curves than 61C anneal/extend. It is definitely worth revisiting increased anneal/extend temps across a concentration range of target on the tongue swab device. Since previous optimization has only been performed at high target concentration, the impact on specificity and sensitivity was likely masked.

Dry Chip Sensitivity
In the previous experiment, using Ftaq batch D0411 at a 1:5 dilution, we increased the Ftaq concentration (plus trehalose and BSA) by 20% and 40% from the normal formulation, which improved Ftaq sensitivity at 66 copies/channel.
We then repeated the experiment using batch D1212 at a 1:40 dilution (since the Ftaq concentration in this batch is higher than that of batch D0411); however, the results appeared inconsistent.
Before proceeding further with experiments to improve Ftaq sensitivity, in my opinion it would be better to develop a quantitative or semi-quantitative polymerase calculation method. This would facilitate future enzyme development, both in liquid and dry formats. If the enzyme source has a consistent concentration, the results of other research activities will also be more consistent. Ode has experience in semi-quantitative protein quantification from his research prior to joining Formulatrix, and he may be able to try applying it again (just a small suggestion from my side).
c) Scale-up of dry chip production
On January 8, 2026, we will evaluate a production scenario from Munis by increasing output from 18 chips/day to 36 chips/day, and assess it using a systematic interval sampling method to determine whether this scale-up impacts dry chip quality.

Demonstrating the specificity risk of allowing an fTaq reaction that was preheated to 85C to slowly cool to 60C before PCR cycling. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 85C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to slowly cool to 60C (similar to current tongue swab device preheat sequence) results in high levels of non-specific primer-dimer amplification and no target-specific amplification -l ink. On the tongue swab device, the preheated fTaq reaction should not be allowed to cool below 85C prior to PCR cycling. 

Preliminary check on preheating to 85C during mixing. 
Previously, we have started to use 65C during preheating. When the temperature was raised to 85C and tested on dried chips, it flatlined. The chips from the same batch performed fine when the preheating temperature was set to 65C. Link to journal entry here .  


Preheating set at 65 C
	
	
Preheating set at 85 C
	
	


Since this was done with dried chips, there was a slight doubt on whether the dried assay in the channel is somehow affected negatively when the heatpad is ramping up its temperature. The dried assay is not directly on top of the heatpad, but 85C is relatively high and the dried assay is not too far from the heatpad. To proof this, the runs were repeated with liquid reactions with the preheating set to 65C and 85C. Turns out, a similar pattern was observed, where the reactions that had a higher preheating temperature performed very poorly.  Ignore the difference in reactions per chip for the dried test shown above to the liquid test shown below. The dried test used leftover chips that were made for dilution tests for a previous batch. 

Preheating set at 65 C
	
	
Preheating set at 85 C
	
	


This leads me to believe that the higher temperature somehow negatively affects the components in the mixture.This results are also different from what was observed in FUSA, where preheating (up to 90C) improves specificity (judged by meltcurves). It is worth noting that there are a few differences between FUSA’s experiments and ours : 
fTaq is added after the preheating in FUSA. Here, the mix already contains all components since we cannot add anything after the chip is sealed.
FUSA uses a qPCR machine instead of TS device. 
Most of the data uses commercial Taq. 

Technically, all the components that we use (probes and primers, buffer, and  dNTPs, even the fTaq) are thermostable at 85C, or even higher. I am not sure how the combination of using our TS device (instead of a regular qPCR machine) and preheating for a brief moment causes the performance to become significantly worse. More tests needs to be done to understand what we can improve if we want to incorporate a preheating step. 

Is it the fTaq that is negatively affected by the heating that causes weak activity? Unlikely since taq is arguably more thermostable compared to all the other components. 
Other components somehow cannot handle the heat? If so, why? They’re all fine at 95C during the denaturation step. The preheating step does not take too long either. 
Is the liquid heating up too rapidly in the channel during the mixing step?
If the issue is heat-caused damage, it’s worth trying to add more sugar based enhancers. Most of them are known to act as stabilizers and thermoprotectants. We already use trehalose. Sucrose is another option.

Adding Evagreen to TS tests.
Link to report 
The initial idea to add evagreen is to gauge the amount of nonspecific amplicons that’s produced when reactions are run in the TS device, especially when we use low copy gblocks. I ran some tests with and without fluorescence, with and without evagreen to see how it affected the baseline. 

In the buffer only runs, addition of evagreen did not increase the baseline significantly. However, when it is added to reactions that contains assays, the baseline increases. And the curve amplitude increases significantly in just the first few cycles of the log phase. This could indicate that there’s a lot of non-specific amplification happening and the evagreen is binding to all the double stranded nucleotides. It is also important to note that the oversaturation is caused by the relatively low limit of our lenses. It would be interesting to see if other polymerase other than fTaq (especially hotstart ones) has the same reactions

fTaq Sensitivity Improvement for Low Copy templates w/ DMSO
When using DMSO for almost all of my previous experiments, the amount used was always 2% for all template concentration. This amount was based on this testing, where the addition 2% DMSO had the most promising result when compared to 1-3%. However, I feel like that should have been explored more as the addition of 2% DMSO in  the upcoming tests did not help much with lower copies (660 and below). 

Another test was done with gradient levels of DMSO ranging from 2, 4 and 6% with 660 copies of templates (since this seems to be the cutoff for sensitivity). The experiment was repeated with annealing set at 55C and 57.5C. It appears that 4% DMSO increases PCR performance better as opposed to the original 2%, with the annealing set at 57.5 C for 660 copies of template. Between the two chosen annealing temperature, it is not quite clear which one is better, or if I can still tinker around with it (increasing it by one degree perhaps). The use of 57.5C is slightly better with the current amount of tests done when compared to 55C, but it has greater run-to-run variability. Here’s a comparison between the 2 temperatures with varying amount of DMSO with 660 copies of template (IS6110 FAM channel only). 
When the same set-up is tried for even lower copies (66 copies), similar enhancement was not observed. This can be explained by the fact that out fTaq has reached its limit, or there are some more alterations that can be done. The curve quality that is observed with 66copies is mostly flat, with some curves. Link to report : 

Prior to this, I tried to combine adding  2%DMSO and additional fTaq to see if that would increase performance, since the 2%DMSO only would not help much when we are processing TS samples. The results did not show any significant improvement. Link to report : . 
I also did baseline testing for fTaq + DMSO on QS6, it  can be found here   

For the following week, I will be focusing on trying to reproduce the results with dried fTaq using formula 2, where I will do a LoD test for dried fTaq, and then attempt to see if DMSO in the resuspension buffer could increase sensitivity as it did with the liquid tests.

Sensitivity improvement
I understand that increasing the amount of enzyme means increasing the amount of preservative reagent as well. However, in this experiment, I only increased the amount of enzyme to determine the limit of the preservative reagent’s ability to maintain the enzyme. In this experiment, we can see that adding 20% more enzyme without increasing the preservative reagent improves the curve quality. However, after increasing the enzyme by 40% and then 60%, the curve quality drops significantly. This occurs because the amount of preservative reagent is no longer sufficient.  

If we want to increase the amount of polymerase, we also need to increase the preservative reagents, The preservative reagent must be kept at the concentration :  trehalose 1 M at 9.5% of the total dried volume, and BSA 10 mg/mL at 10.8% of the total dried volume. In the next experiment, I will increase the concentration of the preservative reagent in FTAq by adding 40% and 60% concentration increments.and then test it at a lower gBlock concentration.

CPB (GMP for Medical Devices)
The Ministry of Health of the Republic of Indonesia has implemented a new regulation regarding the certification process, where CPAKB has changed its name to CPB (Cara Pembuatan yang Baik)
Summary
About the Technical Responsibility Person (PJT). All classification details before PERMENKES-11-2025.pdf are No longer in effect / No longer valid. So the technical Responsibility Person(PJT) classification just to be simple as just having a minimum education degree; "A Diploma degree (D3) in technical, science, or medical fields." but must still have certification for Technical responsibility person CPB.
There is a new update regarding the certification application process. Previously, there were no limits on additional data submissions. However, a limitation has now been implemented: a maximum of 2 submissions during the document review process and 2 submissions for CAPA/CAR fulfillment.

Ibeng Bandung visit
In Thursday morning I visited Prof Bhacti company. (Pakar Biomedika) and meet their RnD representatives Anita.

Small company of 20-ish people that produces rapid test kit for many diseasses and narcotics.
Have ISO 13485, CPAKB, CPAKD, and some of their product TKDN certified.
Facilities isn’t that impressive, lab is not particularly clean room either, but well organized
But their production use humidity controlled room that keep the humidity level below 30% to keep the quality of their dryed reagent
Their RnD is only on formulation, others part is mostly imported.
I asked her with this much variety of products, how hard is it to acquire izin edar. She said it’s quite simple, just product specification, clinical trial document, SOP, etc. But there are no minimum sensitivity or specivity.
She said that TKDN is not simple and there are indication of collusion because even when their company develop and manufacture the products locally, their TKDN percentage is not as high as their hope. But other company that only do repackaging of imported products can get higher TKDN.
When visiting their lab, there is electrical blackout for quite long time, and their refrigerator with a lot of expensive chemicals also turn off but they don’t seem worried at all. I just find that funny.
After that, around 11am I go to Pusat Akademik, Inovasi, Teknologi, dan Riset Kesehatan Universitas Padjadjaran (Centre of Academics, Innovation, Technology, and Health Research of Unpad). Just across the street from Labkesda Jabar (one of lab for izin edar verification) and Hasan Sadikin Hospital (Main hospital at West Java) to meet Prof Bhacti and his team. Also it’s near FORB, only around 4km.

It’s a research centre that also have clinic that specializes for TB and HIV patient. So they always have samples ready.
Their TB work is mostly funded by Gates foundation
They are testing the devices that are sponsored by Gates foundation so it can be adopted by government ASAP. Pluslife is their first success, but I see they also test other devices, currently testing portnat (link) and I see one box from other manufacturer waiting in queue for testing.
Discussion with Prof Bhacti and his team

They are very interested in our company, especially after I showed them our line of products. They keep asking if we can make costum machine.
The TB diagnostic test market is surely crowded right now. We need an angle to stand out from the rest. He said the TB test plus rif resistance testing with low price sure can be an advantage.
Prof Bhacti is the one that write testing standard regulation for izin edar. Pluslife got izin edar before he write the rules. He said sorry but it’s better than try to get WHO PQ.
It looks like MoH really listen to their opinion and recommendation. Many of their study is to shape the TB policy nationally.
Even when I’m there, I can’t meet with Bonny beacuse she had a meeting with MoH peoples. Asking for her opinion.
He said that the minister of health hates genexpert because of their high prices, monopoly, and being expensive to maintain (He is actually an economic guy before becoming minister) 
He suggested that we look into multipanel respiratory diagnostics, because right now none made with TB included. Private hospitals using them and the price isn’t very restricted.
Portnat

They said that portnat test is near completion for phase 1 trial, around 500 samples done from 600ish target.
Portnat is simpler and easier to use compare to pluslife. But not as simple as our workflow.

The sensitivity is not as good as pluslife (on their website their LoD is 400CFU/ml)
During the trial, they said portnat had a problem in the device, so the manufacturer swap the devices mid trial.
Portnat aim to be POC devices, super compact, easy to use.
Interestingly the user still prefer genexpert compared to pluslife or portnat. Because after preparing and inserting the cartridge into the device, there are no more extra steps to do, just wait for result.
Pluslife Info
Government does pluslife trial at Jogja maybe to seek second opinion, it’s done and they already bought 100 pluslife devices to spread in West and Central Java
Prof Bhacti team is dividing the pluslife trial into 3 phase, 1st phase is 600 samples test in their lab, 2nd phase is run the pluslife at puskesmas and compare to genexepert, final phase is send 20 pluslife to 20 puskesmas but not necessarily to compare with other devices, just to see the acceptance of doctors and lab workers.
For 3rd phase Prof Bhacti said that they buy the cartridges themself, with discounted prices
The devices can run without the software, just insert the cartridge and run, there are led light to indicate the result. The device saved result and can be check when connecting to the software.

User can manually insert patient data into the software by comparing to the code at the sticker on cartridge with run history

The pluslife they are using is run on docking station for 5 devices. The laptop only need to connect to the docking station and automatically detect all connected devices.

They said that they still can’t use the device using smartphone yet.
The thermolyse can run 3 samples at once.

3.2 Research Design The research design to be used is descriptive observational, namely research that describes a condition or problem explored through field observations (Sugiyono, 2018). The research subjects are selected using purposive sampling, or sample selection based on specific criteria. The inclusion criteria in this study include:
Research subjects are suspected pulmonary TB patients aged ≥19 years, both male and female,
Research subjects with TB symptoms based on a doctor’s diagnosis. TB symptoms include low-grade fever, cough lasting >2 weeks, weight loss, and night sweats,
Research subjects include both outpatients and inpatients at RSPAW,
Research subjects have obtained TCM GeneXpert MTB Rif Ultra results from sputum or bronchial lavage samples within a 0–7 day range and have not received anti-TB drugs (OAT) during this period.
The exclusion criteria in this study are:
Research subjects fall into the category of MDR-TB or XDR-TB patients,
Subjects unwilling to participate in the study.
The sputum of patients with these symptoms will be examined using TCM GeneXpert MTB Rif/Ultra. This study is single-blinded, meaning that the researchers from the Formulatrix team do not know the patients’ TCM GeneXpert results before examining and releasing the results from the tongue swab.
The number of patients required for this study is 20 patients (with tongue swabs collected twice from each patient), with the sputum TCM results consisting of 10 positive samples and 10 negative samples.

3.3.1. Participant Selection Participants in this study are patients with suspected TB symptoms based on a doctor’s diagnosis at RSPAW. Participants have undergone TCM GeneXpert testing by the RSPAW Research Team but have not received OAT therapy during this period. Before participating, patients will be asked to read and sign an informed consent form stating their willingness to participate in the study. The officer who will collect the tongue swab samples will provide an explanation regarding the procedure to be carried out, the purpose and benefits of the research, as well as the rights and obligations of the patients during the research process.

Union conference 2024 Summary (Yosi’s POV)
Detection target
Most TB cases in Indonesia are Rif-sensitive and Inh-resistant.
Iso-marker is important, we can check the WHO database for Indonesia’s mutation
Non MTB detection is also important.
Current GeneXpert testing is not completely fit for testing in all  countries.
IVD tools
Genes2Me: OnePCR – Fully Automated Detection System
Separate reagent well inside one cartridge. Controlled using liquid handling inside the device. Extracted DNA will be transferred to a new vial containing lyop assay, then amplified.
Truenat - Molbio
All consumables were prepared.
New device for sonication, called Truenat lyse. Claimed to be able to process tongue swab samples, but still under validation process.	
Coyote Bioscience
FlashDetect Nano
Sleek looking device, cartridge size similar to genexpert’s. 
Several line detections (respiratory, TB, HPV, HIV, etc)
Intelligenome
Crispr-pcr based diagnostic tools. Still unclear whether it uses blood- or sputum/saliva as the sample. Consumables look similar to a retractable pen. There’s a membrane for catching DNA (incubation 15 min) then reaction with crispr-pcr reagent for another 15 min. Result based on fluorescence intensity
Illumina - Genoscreen
Sequence based analysis to detect 15 antibiotics resistance genes, mixture amplicons. Sequencing after pcr, then detect each antibiotic resistance-related gene.
There are several portable X-ray devices.
Yosi’s impression and questions:
Where do we want to sell Stampede?
If we focus on selling in Indonesia, we might need to alter our target genes. We can add a disclaimer on our product that Stampede is made in Indonesia, designed for Indonesia.
Which market are we targeting?
Screening test - portable, fast, not necessarily need to have too many target genes 
First line Diagnostic test - more comprehensive testing to find first line antibiotics resistance. 
Let’s focus on one key point for our device. More development of additional features for the next version.
Rather than trying to beat other IVD that are on the market, let’s focus on being the best of our version. 
If we keep wanting to pursue technologies currently available on the market, we won’t be able to produce even a single device.
We need to be more active in attending seminars/conferences/training/exhibitions, sending the suitable and relevant people for the occasion.
One idea to speed up development research from the science part, is to collaborate with universities. 
PRO: able to get TB strains (might be easier than we try to bet it by ourself), have more man power to do experiments (from those who are doing their thesis project), experimental results can be published and be a baseline for validation process.
CONS:Higher budget, possibility to have a co-collaborator option (are we ready to share the fame?), might take time to setup from proposal to real experimental stage.

Kalgen meeting topics:
Manufacturing discussion and factory tour. Can they tell us about their reagent manufacturing? What do they make in-house? How long did it take to learn the processes and build the infrastructure?
Regulatory experience? Can they tell us about the process they went through to achieve Ijin Edar for their TB test? Can they give us any advice or connect us with anyone at the national MoH?
Discuss FMLX TB test. Emphasize that our test will not directly compete with them because ours is a point of care test and theirs is a lab test. We can still be friends 🙂.
Once we finish the general discussion about our companies and TB tests, then we can talk about collaboration:
Co-marketing
Kalgen and Formulatrix go to market as partners. “The Kalgen TB Test, powered by Formulatrix.” We would make instruments and cartridges. Kalgen would provide assays, manage regulatory approval, and handle sales and marketing. We would split revenue based on our responsibilities.
Reagent supply
Formulatrix would contract Kalgen as their exclusive or non-exclusive reagent supplier. We could create a supply agreement with a non-binding forecast for a few years. We would pay a fair price to Kalgen for their reagents.
Consulting
Formulatrix would hire Kalgen to give guidance and support to get our TB test onto the market in Indonesia. We would pay a reasonable fee for their time and expertise.
Future assay development
Formulatrix would contract Kalgen scientists to develop new assays for our testing platform. We would pay an up-front fee plus milestone payments for validated assays.

We got an interesting idea to use the Planar Transformer that was presented by Asfan in this document. The key feature of this transformer are:
Small dimension can save the PCB layout area
High efficiency
Suitable for high frequency switching
Low noise level by minimizing the electromagnetic interference generated during operation.
Production scale

If the L is too high caused by the material permeability (µ), the flux density (B) will dramatically increase even with a small amount of current (I). Once the B reaches the limit of material, the core will be saturated immediately.

Trace dimension should be considered based on the maximum temperature increase depending on the power needed. We can choose 2oz for a high power application sonicator. The clearance between traces should be considered if the switching voltage is quite high.
The main concern on this planar design is the proximity effect. The current on the one layer will repel current in the other layer so it will increase the AC resistance. To avoid this we should design the planar transformer with interleaved stack for primary and secondary. The best practice is : pri (top layer)-sec (inner layer 1)-pri (inner layer 2)-sec (inner layer 3)-pri (inner layer 4)-sec (inner layer 5) - pri (bottom layer). This interleaving layer stacking will cancel out the magnetic field on each layer then will decrease the proximity effect and the leakage inductance.

01/15/2026
Prototyping ZTP-315 infrared amphenol temperature sensor.
The sensor is already received, and we start making the signal conditioning. The output of the sensor is voltage relative to the temperature and not very linear. The voltage level is very small, at 80 C, the output voltage is around 11mVolt. The maximum remote sensing is 120C. To make it can be read by the microcontroller ADC, it needs to be amplified. Datasheet.
This sensor also integrated with an internal NTC thermistor to sense the ambient temperature to compensate for the IR temperature reading at the calculation.

Fig 1. Sensor and its response

The amplification can be set to match with the maximum ADC voltage, for example if we need to make the 80 deg have a 3.3V output we can use amplification 300x. In this test we use the instrument amplifier INA826 and set the gain around 100x to avoid noise because we use a prototype board. 

Fig 2. Sensor signal conditioning
The signal conditioning has 2 outputs, 1 for thermopile output (infrared sensor), and the second is thermistor for ambient temperature sensor.

For this prototype we use the old stampede controller board that has the INA826 amplifier, and utilize STM32 for the data acquisition.


Fig 3. Prototype setup and sensor attachment on the testing JIG
Temperature conversion from the electrical.
Because this sensor does not have a linear response, to convert the electrical value to the temperature value it can be used a Stefan-Boltzmann equation. For thermopile sensor:

Q = is the heat flux
 = Emissivity
 = Stefan-Boltzmann constant
T = absolute temperature.
For the IR thermopile sensor like ZTP-315 the equation can be rewritten as:

, where
V = voltage output
K = Calibration constant
T = absolute temperature.
To find the value of K we can use some mathematical calculations from voltage output based on some temperature point. In this prototype we refer to this reference and use its K value and apply it to our system. Reference doc.

Fig 4. Variation of K value and its error response.

Testing.
In this prototype the objective is :
To see if the red light LED from the vial heater affect the IR reading or not
How much temperature difference is sensed from the outside vial compared to temperature inside the vial sensed by the thermocouple.

1. Red light LED effect.
The sensor has the infrared wave length response from 5um to 16um

Fig 5. ZTP-315 wave length response vs vial heater LED wave length.
From this data, the LED light shouldn’t affect the IR temperature reading because the wavelength response from the IR sensor is very far compared to the LED red light wavelength. From the testing, it proved that the LED light didn’t affect the IR reading. When the LED OFF the IR sensor reading around 27C, after LED turned ON there is no jumping signal on the temperature reading, and the temperature reading will increase gradually based on the vial temperature during the LED stay ON. Video.
 


Fig 6. LED light didn’t affect the temperature reading.
2. Temperature difference is sensed from the outside vial compared to temperature inside the vial sensed by the thermocouple
In this test we found that the sensor positioning also affects the temperature reading, if the sensor position is not sensing in front of the liquid, the temperature difference compared to the thermocouple is around 25C.


Fig 7. IR sensor position on the top of liquid surface

If the sensor position is in the liquid area, the temperature difference between the IR sensor and the thermocouple is around 5C


Fig 8. IR sensor position on the liquid area
By this, we can see that the IR sensor can be used for sensing the liquid temperature for heating the vial because the LED light didn’t affect the temperature reading by the IR, but further testing should be done to get a good sensor positioning, calculate the K value to have good sensing precision.

Other next to do is testing together with the vial cap heater and do some bang bang testing controller by temperature data and compare the result with the old method that uses a load cell data for bang bang controller.

Ultrasonic Driver Board Preparation.
The RD3.0.0 board has already arrived and finished from soldering. Have an issue with the inductor production, 2 of 7 inductors produced arcing and plasma when the driver turned on and caused transducer failure and getting hot.

Fig 1. Inductor produce an arcing and plasma smell
Our assumption is that there is a part of the litz wires that the insulator was damaged, when high voltage occurs it will create a corona discharge and disturb the magnetic field inside the inductor core. Then we disassemble the inductor to add additional varnish (corona dope) to ensure that all of the litz wire is covered by this insulator.
 
Fig 2. Pouring the inductor winding with additional a corona dope varnish
After waiting around 24 hours for curing, then assemble the inductor with the core and test using LCR meter and in the driver.
One of the inductors seems to be shorted because when measured with the LCR meter its shows only 9uH (should be 2mH) and the other one the inductance value is still fine.

Fig 3. Test 2 inductors with LCR meter after adding additional insulator varnish.
But when tested on the driver, it seems like the inductor can’t transfer the current to the transducer and the waveform outputs on the excitation voltage only square wave + harmonic.

Fig 4. Test 2 inductors with the driver after adding additional insulator varnish.
So by this we assume that there are many layers of winding that might have an issue with the litz wire insulation and adding the additional varnish corona dope didn’t help it to cover the damaged insulator. For now we have 4 working inductors and will produce another inductor for sparepart.

Fig 5. Stock for the inductor, one of them (Yellow) has larger inductance so we can’t utilize it.
Test the driver with a working inductor.
During testing the driver, we also found that adding more capacitor parallel with the burden resistor in the current transformer can shift the phase compared to the excitation. The effect is it can reduce the current consumption (3.5A) but still have the same cavitation if compared with 5A.
This phenomena is still under debugging, because increasing a capacitor will lower the total impedance of the burden. If we follow the ohm law where V = I * Z, the output voltage should be lower if Z is decreasing.




But there will be another analysis, the Z itself will not be pure resistive. If we calculate the Z based on the RC parallel we can get:




But if we consider the leakage inductance, the impedance and phase shift now will be affected by the inducatnce.



Where the XC is the capacitive reactance. If we take an example, if the C is 1nF at 35KHz frequency the reactance will be around 6KOhm, and if we increase the capacitance to 100nF the  reactance will be around 63KOhm. At 1nF the Z will be dominated by the R burden, but at the 100nF capacitance the Z will be affected by the reactive and resistive and thus will affect the phase as well.
Here is for 1nF C filter combined with the 100 Ohm burden:
Video.

Video.

But what I'm thinking is, because the feedback interacts with the RC charge and discharge, the phase shift may affect how the switching changes based on that phase margin.


Fig 8. Feedback path
So maybe the phase shift has an effect like indirectly acting like duty cycle, if the phase margin is higher (180deg) the duty maybe can be say like 50%, and near to 0 deg its like lowering the duty. Decreasing the transformer ratio can reduce the current draw since the reflected secondary side impedance to the primary side is lower and vice versa. So by this, there are 2 assumption :
We must set the reflected secondary side impedance in correct value, so the power and mechanical conversion could be effective
Too or too high reflected impedance will sacrifice the electro-mechanical conversion.
Temperature ramp-up plot for each configuration with measurement setup:
Duty : 0.5s
Off delay: 1s
Cycle count: 25
Data here.

Fig 2. Temperature ramp-up plot
From those plots, assumption 2 may be correct because the lower and higher transformer ratio take the lower ramp-up rate. 
From here, we still not can determine the correct transformer ratio and inductor inductance value but we will the highest (2mH L and 3.06 Ratio) and the middle (3mH L and 3.06Ratio or 2mH L and 2.12 Ratio), we will continue test and also fishing the JIG first due the sonicator tip was not perpendicular to the vial.

For IDEA 3, in the current driver circuit we can’t do a duty cycle adjustment because the driver uses a push-pull topology. We need to modify the driver to use full bridge topology and change the gate driver so the duty cycle can be applied to the circuit.
